abstract: --idea theory admits a clean mathematical axiomatization. This formalization establishes the foundational type class, derives key theorems, and verifies everything in Lean 4 with Mathlib.

Read the source document at /Users/halleyyoung/Documents/formalize/IDT_Theory.pdf and build upon / strictly enhance its mathematical content (do not write the file path into generated artifacts).

tasks.title: Lean theorems: Idea Structures
tasks.description:
Create lean/IdeaTheory/Theorems1.lean for 'Idea Structures', a SUBSTANTIAL Lean 4 file (target 600–1500 lines) developing the formal theory of Idea Structures for '--idea theory' from the ground up.  Import Foundations and any sibling Theorems files you need.  Place everything inside `namespace IdeaTheory`.  Zero `sorry`, zero `admit`, zero `native_decide`, zero `#check`.
REQUIRED ARTIFACTS (each block must be present and non-empty):
  1. FILE-LEVEL DOCSTRING: a multi-paragraph `/-! … -/` module      docstring (250–500 words) explaining (a) what part of the      informal literature on '--idea theory' this file formalizes, (b) which      authors/works the development draws on, (c) the design decisions      made (which definitions are taken as primitive, what conventions      differ from the literature), (d) a roadmap of the file's main      theorems and how they depend on each other, and (e) the role this      file plays inside Volume 1 of the project.
  2. AUXILIARY DEFINITIONS: at least 8–15 `def`/`structure`/`abbrev`      declarations specific to Idea Structures, each with its own `/-- … -/`      docstring naming the informal antecedent in the literature.
  3. HELPER LEMMA LAYER: at least 40–120 small named lemmas with      full `by` proofs.  Group them into clearly labeled `section`      blocks (e.g. `section Closure`, `section Monotonicity`, …).       Every section opens with a `/-! ## Section title … -/` comment      describing what it establishes and why.
  4. THREE HEADLINE THEOREMS named `theorem_1_1`,      `theorem_1_2`, `theorem_1_3`.  Each MUST carry a      `/-- … -/` docstring of 120–300 words that:
        (a) names the informal claim from the existing non-formalized             literature on '--idea theory' that this theorem formalizes,
        (b) cites the source(s) (Author, Year, Work),
        (c) lists every previously-stated lemma the proof depends on,
        (d) notes any place the formal statement sharpens,             generalizes, restricts, or outright contradicts the             informal version, and
        (e) sketches the proof strategy in 4–8 numbered steps.
     Each headline theorem's actual `by` proof must be a multi-step      tactic block (target 30–150 lines per theorem) using `have`,      `obtain`, `refine`, `calc`, `induction`, `cases`, etc., with      inline comments at every non-trivial step.
  5. AT LEAST 4 COROLLARIES of the headline theorems, named      `corollary_1_*`, each with a docstring linking it to a      downstream informal application discussed in the literature.
  6. EXAMPLES & SANITY CHECKS: at least 6 `example` declarations      exhibiting the headline theorems on concrete inputs.
  7. END-OF-FILE INDEX: a closing `/-! ## Index of results -/` block      listing every public lemma/theorem/corollary in the file with a      one-line summary.
WORKFLOW: write the file in 6–10 incremental batches.  After every batch (≈100–200 lines), save the file and run `lake build` (or `lean --make`) on it; only continue once it compiles green.  Never paste shell or copilot transcript into the file — the file must end exactly with `end IdeaTheory` and contain only valid Lean.
VOLUME CONTEXT: this file belongs to Volume 1; align its emphasis with that volume's theme.

tasks.title: TeX ch (V1): Foundations and Axioms
tasks.description:
Create tex/vol1/ch1.tex for 'Foundations and Axioms' (Volume 1: Foundations): a SUBSTANTIAL monograph chapter (target 25–60 PDF pages, i.e. 8000–18000 words of body LaTeX) bridging the non-formalized literature on '--idea theory' to the machine-verified Lean 4 theorems.  Use theorem/definition/lemma/proof/remark/example environments defined in preamble.sty.  REQUIRED structure (each section must be present and substantively populated):
  1. \chapter{Foundations and Axioms} with a \label, an \epigraph      drawn from the literature, and a 200–300 word      introductory paragraph that frames the chapter's scope.
  2. \section{Background from the Literature} — a      substantial narrative (1500–3000 words) surveying what      the existing non-formalized literature on '--idea theory' says      about this chapter's topic.  Cite at least 8–15 real      works from references.bib via \cite{key}.  Cover      (a) historical origins, (b) competing definitions and      schools of thought, (c) central informal claims,      (d) folklore results and unproven conjectures, (e) open      questions.  Use multiple \subsection{} headings.
  3. \section{From Informal Claims to Formal Statements} —      800–1500 words explaining how each informal claim above      is rendered as a precise Lean 4 statement.  Include a      long translation table (\begin{tabular}, ≥ 10 rows)      with columns (Informal claim, Source, Formal theorem,      Notes on divergence).  Discuss the design choices that      went into the formalization.
  4. \section{Definitions} — at least 6–10      \begin{definition} blocks, each followed by a      \begin{remark} (50–150 words) connecting it to the      informal antecedent and noting any deviation.
  5. \section{Main Results} — at least 5–8      \begin{theorem} blocks, each followed by a real      \begin{proof} of 200–600 words (not placeholders)      structured as numbered steps; interleave      \begin{lemma} declarations as needed and prove them      too.  Add at least 3 \begin{corollary} blocks with      proofs.
  6. \section{Worked Examples} — at least 3      \begin{example} blocks (200–400 words each) showing      the theorems in action.
  7. \section{Lean 4 Verification} — one or more      \begin{lstlisting}[language=Lean4] blocks reproducing      the actual headline theorem statements from      lean/IdeaTheory/Theorems*.lean, plus 300–600      words of accompanying prose explaining how the Lean      proof realizes the informal argument.
  8. \section{Discussion: What the Formalization Reveals} —      500–1000 words reflecting on what the formal proofs      clarify, simplify, sharpen, or refute about the informal      literature.  Cite at least 4 further works.
  9. \section{Open Problems} — 200–400 words listing 4–8      concrete open problems left by both the literature and      the formalization.
 10. \section{Notes and References} — bibliographic notes      guiding the reader to the most important sources cited.
Use \cref / \Cref throughout for cross-references; cite generously; ensure every theorem/definition/lemma carries a \label.  No placeholder TODOs in the final output.
